ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for murtabak; mapped to flatbread food product as the closest defensible ancestor. Murtabak is a pan-fried stuffed flatbread.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated murtabak code; A0CKP (Pancakes and similar products) is the closest defensible category for a pan-fried flour-based stuffed flatbread.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for murtabak or equivalent stuffed flatbread in the clinical terminology.

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of vegetable murtabak is approximately 150 g (one piece), as commonly sold at Singapore hawker stalls and food courts.

nutrient_source.title: Murtabak (vegetable) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Murtabak (vegetable), CR ID F05020067. All seven reported nutrients (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, dietary fibre) are sourced at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate based on stuffed flatbread / roti canai category
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for vegetable murtabak exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 66 is estimated from the closest analogous food: roti canai / plain paratha (pan-fried wheat flour flatbread), which has a reported GI of approximately 62–70 (medium) in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. The vegetable filling (peas, carrot, corn, onion) and egg contribute fibre and protein that moderately lower the glycaemic response relative to a plain flatbread. A midpoint GI of 66 is used. Glycemic load per serving = (66 × 19.62 / 100) × 1.5 portions equivalent = 19.4 g per 150 g serving. The input record's GI of 15 (low) from an 'Embedded GI Database' was rejected as it is not an approved source and is implausibly low for a wheat-flour pan-fried flatbread with 19.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 376 mg per 100 g — borderline high; typical 150 g serving delivers approximately 564 mg sodium, representing about 25% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to one piece and avoid high-sodium dipping sauces such as curry gravy or chilli sauce, which can substantially increase total sodium intake per meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated GI of 66 (medium) and carbohydrate of 19.6 g per 100 g; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 29 g available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 19.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one piece per meal, pair with a non-starchy vegetable side to lower the overall glycaemic response, and avoid sweetened dipping sauces that add rapidly absorbed sugars.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pan-frying in oil contributes to total fat of 10.2 g per 100 g; saturated fat and trans fat content from frying oil are not reported by HPB but are a concern for cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request preparation with minimal oil, limit portion to one piece, and avoid high-fat curry dipping sauces; the vegetable filling provides beneficial dietary fibre (2.1 g per 100 g) that partially offsets cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pan-frying generates pro-inflammatory advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); trans fat from frying oil is strongly pro-inflammatory.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise consumption of pan-fried murtabak; if consumed, request preparation with minimal oil and retain the vegetable filling for its anti-inflammatory fibre content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Vegetable murtabak is a popular Singaporean hawker dish consisting of a thin wheat flour dough that is pan-fried on a hot plate and filled with a savoury mixture of egg, green peas, carrot, sweet corn, and onion. The outer dough is crispy and golden from the hot-plate cooking, while the interior is soft, moist, and flavourful from the vegetable and egg filling. It is a vegetarian variant of the classic murtabak, which traditionally uses minced meat. Murtabak is of Arab-Indian culinary origin and is widely sold at Malay and Indian Muslim hawker stalls across Singapore, Malaysia, and Indonesia. It is commonly eaten as a breakfast item or snack and is typically served with a curry dipping sauce or pickled onions. Nutritionally, a 100 g portion provides 190 kcal, 4.96 g protein, 10.2 g fat, 19.62 g carbohydrate, 2.06 g dietary fibre, and 376 mg sodium. The moderate carbohydrate content and vegetable filling make it a reasonable choice for most adults in moderate portions, but the sodium level and frying oil content require caution for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is 66 (medium), placing it in the moderate glycaemic load category for a standard serving.
common_user_queries: Can I eat murtabak if I have high blood pressure?
What is the GI of murtabak?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F05020067) at confidence 0.95, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. The GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest defensible flatbread analogue (roti canai / paratha), downgraded from exact to analogous_estimate. Overall record confidence is high for macros and medium for GI.
notes: Fields set to -1 (data not available from HPB source): saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Reason: the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Murtabak (vegetable) reports only energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, sugar, sodium, and dietary fibre; detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrients, and moisture data are not published for this entry as of April 2026. The input record's GI value of 15 (low) attributed to an 'Embedded GI Database' was rejected as this is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2 source policy, and a GI of 15 is implausibly low for a pan-fried wheat flour flatbread with 19.6 g carbohydrate per 100 g. A defensible analogous estimate of GI 66 (medium) from the University of Sydney GI Database (roti canai / paratha category) has been substituted and documented. No core macro is zero; all four core macros (energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate) are positive and sourced from HPB.
